goal: Alpha walkthrough hardening: fresh install to first sealed intent

summary.title: Alpha walkthrough hardening: three friction fixes + audit doc, no new features
summary.what: Mode shift per user direction. Ran the full daily-commands path as a fresh user (mkdir → git init → init → start → append → seal → log/show/trace → status), captured every interaction, found three confidence-eroding bugs and fixed them inline, captured three medium-priority follow-ups in docs_for_ai/alpha-walkthrough-friction.md, confirmed five non-issues survived the audit. F1: ^mainline: init added to default skip patterns so fresh repos do not show the init commit as uncovered. F2: collectUnsealedDrafts cross-references the view so a draft file claiming sealed_local for a view-merged intent does not produce contradictory output blocks and a stale resume suggestion. F3: bare mainline init prints a visible warning when --actor-name is omitted. One regression test locks in F2.
summary.why: User explicit direction: "现在不该继续加命令了。下一步应该从继续扩功能切到验证 v0.1 是否真的能被别人用。" The previous PRs (trace, status daily entry point, status auto-sync) had completed the daily-loop closure. Adding more commands risked degrading signal quality. The right next move is hardening — validate that a new user reaches first sealed intent following only on-screen prompts, find the friction, fix what is small, document the rest.
summary.user_goal: Make mainline credible on first use: walk through it as a new user, find what makes them give up, fix the small things now, file the medium things for later.

decisions.point: Fix all friction inline vs split into multiple PRs
decisions.chose: fix three small items inline; document the rest
decisions.rationale: Each of F1/F2/F3 is a one-or-two-file change with no schema risk and a focused test. Bundling them as "alpha hardening" matches the audit framing and the three-fixes one-doc shape is easy to review. F4-F6 (medium) need separate product calls.

decisions.chose: view authoritative for terminal states only
decisions.rationale: View merged/abandoned/superseded/reverted are TERMINAL — the draft file cannot legitimately claim those states from a less-informed perspective. For drafting/sealed_local both sources are valid (draft is local truth before sync rebuilds), so view override only applies once the view has already moved past those states. Surgical not catastrophic.

decisions.point: F3 warning vs hard-error on missing --actor-name
decisions.rationale: Hard-error would break programmatic init flows (test fixtures, CI scaffolding) that rely on the default. Warning preserves the default while making the trade-off visible to humans who type bare `mainline init` themselves.

decisions.point: Friction list in markdown vs structured tracker
decisions.chose: markdown doc adjacent to the spec patches
decisions.rationale: Same audience as the rc6/v0.3 spec patches (LLM implementers and the user). Issue tracker would create a reading-cost split. The doc explicitly distinguishes fixed-inline / captured-followup / out-of-scope / non-issues — same structure as a good roadmap.

rejected.alternative: Auto-update draft file when sync flips intent to merged
rejected.reason: Cross-cutting (would touch every code path that mutates view status). View-overrides-draft at the read site is surgical and equally correct for the symptom.

rejected.alternative: Make the bare-init warning a hard error
rejected.reason: Breaks fixture flows. Warning surfaces the trade-off without removing programmatic use cases.

rejected.alternative: Add F4 (thread=branch on main) and F5 (empty-diff seal --prepare) inline
rejected.reason: Both need product judgment calls beyond mechanical fixes (what should thread default to? when does an empty diff justify a warning vs a hard error?). Documented for follow-up rather than guessed at here.
Pre-rollout hardening should respond to real friction, not anticipated friction. Friend MVP rollout will tell us if the existing 13-line quickstart misses anything.

risks: F2 view-override could mask a future bug where view goes terminal-state but the draft is genuinely still being worked on. Acceptable: view going terminal requires either a sealed event landing on the actor log (cant happen for active drafting) or scanMainNotes seeing a note (cant happen for unsealed). The pre-condition for view-override is structurally exclusive with active local work.
F1 skip pattern is per-config; teams that already have a [mainline.skip] block override the default. Storage backfill helps repos created before this PR; new init writes the new defaults directly.
F3 warning text adds 4 lines to bare-init output. Minor real-estate cost; alternative was hard-error which is worse.
followups: F4: thread default on main branch — small UX call, scope a focused PR.
F5: empty-diff seal --prepare nudge — pair with status that warns if user has uncommitted work.
F6: Suggestions secondary action (mainline trace <active>) for review flows.
After friend MVP rollout: compare actual first-use friction to this audit; resolve real-vs-imagined gaps.
Consider an automated walkthrough harness — same scenario as this PR but as a CI smoke test that asserts the on-screen prompts remain consistent across releases.

architectural_claims: Status's UnsealedDrafts / RecentSealed / Suggestions blocks now share a single source of truth: the view authoritatively decides terminal-state intents regardless of stale draft files.
`mainline init` written commits are first-class skipped (default skip pattern) so the fresh-repo first impression is not a coverage warning.
Bare `mainline init` surfaces the default-actor-name trade-off rather than silently committing the user to a placeholder identity.
behavioral_changes: mainline status no longer surfaces the init commit as uncovered.
mainline status no longer reports a merged-in-view intent as Unsealed nor proposes resuming it.
mainline init without --actor-name prints a visible warning + recommendation.
Default [mainline.skip] patterns include ^mainline: init (also backfilled for pre-v0.3 configs).
